item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept of an ancient Foundation being created by who we consider to be Pantheon of Greek gods, who monitored and protected the ancient world from their mythological anomalies. What a way to introduce Classic Mythology into the universe in a way that fits! Having the containment procedure be not anything physical, but instead enforced through Socratic Dialogue is a fun idea too!
A very hearty +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scholar Eternity Collective? Damn, thought I was original with my name for both me and an idea I have. Should I change it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but like this article a lot, from the clever format screw to the "historical foundation" trope being actually well done. My only minor gripes are as follows: the discovery part of the file seems like padding and the opportunity to end the article on the highest note is squandered by following it with a keter duty Heracles joke, which isn't bad, but would fit better as a second or third example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …that's pretty genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized the 'best' answer to this question:

+ show block
– hide block

The ancient facility used to be a real-life place. It suffered a catastrophic containment breach, and they activated the 'breaking of chains' protocol, which took everything to the realm of thought. This is why all of the creatures described are now mythology; the containment procedure turned them from living creatures to bits of mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, it's safe to infer that the aberrations got out, considering none were found in their rooms?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seeing my own name scared the hell out of me! +1

Really though I love the ancient greek scp foundation presented in a Dialogue like this, brilliantly done!
I'm very happy to see this get to 400 votes. Thanks to those who upvoted this Platonic thingamajigy, I really appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely love it. Solid +1. The idea of the ancient Greeks and their gods "containing" the beings of myth as anomalies is fantastic by itself. Turning it into a Socratic dialogue? And not only that, but making it the method of accessing the containment areas? Absolutely phenomenal.

item.title: Incredible
item.content:encoded: I had low expectations for this going in, I'll be honest. I basically skimmed most of it at first. Then when I went back I noticed that little trick in the opening. That's where you got me. Great article, immersive, clever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did intend Iron to represent Neutralized analog, since most of the anomalies contained in Pandora's Box had escaped. It's slightly different from the current Foundation system, though.

That's really interesting about Heracles vs Herakles! I'll have to look it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that Bronze/Silver/Gold/Heroes corresponds to the SEKT system in place here, but what is Iron? It doesn’t appear to correspond to Neutralized.

Also, while I don’t know if this is a stylistic choice or not, “Heracles” is the spelling used for the guy who did the 12 labors. There is actually a separate figure in Greek myth called “Herakles,” who supposedly created the Olympics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can we get an update in which they try to contain 682, ultimately leading to the downfall of their “Foundation.” Not even specifically this, but at least some small allusion to the Hard to Destroy Reptile.

item.title: Re: +304
item.content:encoded: My guy, don't put yourself down like that. There are no guarantees in life, and even if it seems like not everything is going as planned, something will turn up in the end. So no, this won't be your one good SCP entry if you continue to try your best. It will be if you give up now. So don't give up.

Thanks for everyone who's voted for this! I've tried to recreate the magic with other articles, but it looks like this will be my one good SCP entry. Good luck to everyone, especially new authors who are trying to figure stuff out!

item.title: Amazing
item.content:encoded:
I love this idea so much. I thought it was unique, interesting, and just brilliant all-around.
+1

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Being 1/4 Greek IRL:

  • I got the "Pandóra Anesidóra's Box" 1 immediately (bonus points for making it a Píthos-type storage jar like in the original story).
  • I was unsure about Typhoeús/Typhõn/Typhós/Typháon, because I recognized the physical description but couldn't remember the character's name until I was halfway in.
  • I can see why irrational numbers would be considered a real-life cognitohazard, seeing as I hurt my head trying to wrap it around the concept when I myself was introduced to it (I was wondering why they would be in an ancient SCP analogue run by the Gods, though)
  • I recognized Plátōn's Allegory of the Cave, but I was wondering how it tied in (thanks for the clarification). I didn't get the reference to Plátōn's Republic in the article layout, since I had no familiarity with that work outside of the Allegory of the Cave.
  • I never actually read Russel & Whitehead's Principia Mathematica. In fact, this is my 1st time hearing of either that or incompleteness theorem.
  • I will admit that I initially took the "Hēraklẽs on Gold Duty" comment as a callback joke to the formerly ever-present "demoted to Keter Duty" that the SCP Foundation itself used to do to incompetent researchers before the authors got their shit together during the Mass Edit (bear in mind that I was introduced to this franchise after the resulting shitshow died down), but knowing it's a reference to the 12 Labors is even better.

Yes, I'm such a huge nerd that I'm using the transliterated Greek spellings complete with accents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a mythology nerd I just had to give this a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That appears to be the case. I wonder if he will succeed in the termination and/or containment of the twelve uncontained anomalies with the threat of gold. I wish him the best of luck.

In all seriousness, this is a clever scip, I hope the author continues to make works as imaginative as this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rule #2 is that if it's your first time at Meme Club, you have to meme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: congrats on getting that top 5, man!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Format screw made me very uninterested despite the interesting ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Past Foundation but it's X" may very well be a tired trope by now but a containment procedure involving Zeus throwing thunder bolts is cool enough that I didn't mind.

That said, the screw is annoying and felt unnecessary to me, and from a purely "personal taste" perspective reading through the beginning and the philosphical ideal bits were nearly boring enough to stop me before the end. That's on me, though; abstract philosophy just isn't my thing.
Thanks again for the community response. I really appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suppose I can strike "an anomalous Socratic Dialogue that makes everyone talk/write in Socratic dialogue format" from my list of SCP ideas.

+1 for Great Minds and all that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a wonderfully creative idea and I'd to see it explored more. Perhaps some hints as to where exactly all the ancient Greek anomalies are being kept, how they "broke the chains and walked out of the cave", and/or why the format screw is actually necessary. An interview log with a Greek god could potentially be fun too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP has to be one of my favorites, period. +1, here's hoping it gets the slot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is a very, very cool and interesting execution for a dull and cliche idea. I loved the format screw and the whole concept of an object that can only be accessed through philosophical thought, and got even more excited when ideal forms were brought up. And so I was pretty disappointed when the object itself turned out to just be "famous myths and stories but they're skips".

For the containment site, I do like the angle that this is the ideal way to document containment, and maybe the SCP format is so similar because they based it off of this object. But outright naming figures like Pandora and Herakles is just too much for me.

item.title: Note by author
item.content:encoded:
A lot of people have been wondering how the Greeks were able to make this place. While there are already some clues in the text, I added link to SCP-609 (the ontological 6-ball), which has similar properties, to show that this is a thing that can be accomplished on a regular basis.

Edit: I wondered why I started getting a bunch of downvotes after this. Turned out it messed with the iFrame, so I have now deleted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me or is this, the discovery of a previous Foundation held within a philosophical physicality, like the best thing ever. The actual implications of having your thoughts capable of physical interaction is certainly scary, but this work is also just damn intriguing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The explanation for how it ended up this way is in the last 'real' example, Pi Lamba Alpha (for PLAto). It is talking about the allegory of the cave that Plato uses in The Republic, where the reality we see is false, like chained people watching shadow puppets in a cave, and that only by leaving the cave and breaking our chains can we view truth.

Ah right. Yeah I guess I can see that now, but it went completely over my head at the time. I assumed that was one talking about something that made people into those "gods" or something, or maybe their description for breaking the veil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoever wrote this would love existentialcomics.com

You may not win but solely because I love philosophy and laughed a lot while reading this, you deserve 10,000 upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good feedback, thanks. The format is not really inherently required by the anomaly, you're right, and that's one thing I'd like to fix.

The explanation for how it ended up this way is in the last 'real' example, Pi Lamba Alpha (for PLAto). It is talking about the allegory of the cave that Plato uses in The Republic, where the reality we see is false, like chained people watching shadow puppets in a cave, and that only by leaving the cave and breaking our chains can we view truth.

The similarities are more of a joke than how a real ancient foundation would act. The naming system, however, is borrowed from Euclid's Elements, where mathematical objects are referred to by letters.

I agree that this has several issues and I am frankly surprised at the reception it's received, but I'm glad that the idea has resonated with people, because it's one that's tickled my brain for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I am definitely glad to see that my nested collapsible thing has helped someone out, I don't really buy the justification for the format screw here. It's mentioned that is has to be this way due to the nature of the anomaly, but unless I'm really missing something here there's nothing in the anomaly at all that would require this.

The idea itself is cool, though I was expecting an explanation of how this place ended up as a conceptual space when it's implied that it used to physically exist, and that never came, so for me all the examples just felt a bit like a thread of random things that petered out. My other issue is how closely this ancient Foundation staffed by literal greek gods mirrors the current Foundation. I just don't buy that they would designate things with arbitrary groupings of letters instead of big, overblown titles.

I really do like the concept, but ultimately I feel like there's a lot of missed potential here. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not nitpicky at all! I'm still figuring out what makes an SCP 'work' so I'm scared of changing what I have now because I'm pleased with how it's going. After the contest I'll look at all the feedback and make changes that hopefully improve it, and I'll revisit your comment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm no writer, so forgive me if this is dumb, but maybe the philosophers drew Foundation attention just by virtue of holding a public seminar titled Hey We Just Invented A Conceptual Reality, or maybe they streamed it and web-crawlers picked up on something? I don't know, but the "Foundation just happened to have a plant in a super random place" to explain how an SCP was discovered always just bugs me a little. Minor quibble and sorry if that's nitpicky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about 'a college intern' for the foundation, like the way the NSA lets you spend a summer with them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"A Foundation operative planted in the mathematics department of ███████ College"

This was an awesome scip but I think this is the most extreme use of that trope ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite having to stomach the format screw, I really enjoyed reading this. It's very thematic for the contest and well-written overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"All scholars not in the Second Academy that display knowledge of Pi Upsilon Theta are to be drowned at sea."

IMMEDIATE +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll be honest, I think the Allegory of the Cave would have made for a stronger ending than Herakles on Keter duty.

Nevertheless, this was awesome. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This almost feels more like a Wanderer's Library piece than a Foundation piece.

At the same time, it's that one-of-kind "how did nobody try this before?" type of brilliant, and I loved reading every word of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Example 3 is typhon a giant monster that Zeus fought and killed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The format took a little to get used to, but I think it greatly improved the quality of this skip. Love the idea as well! Definitely a + 1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you liked it!

Funny enough, the idea of drowning people who know about irrational numbers is an old urban legend in math circles: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippasus

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course irrational numbers are an SCP. but would the Brotherhood really go to such lengths as to make them a SCP?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Got my vote, cashing in on Percy Jackson fans lol
+1 from me and many more I'm sure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Example 4 is a reference to the Allegory of the Cave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As both a fan of clever format screws and someone who was really into Greek Mythology as a kid, I'm here for this. Well done!
I’m grateful to whoever it was that put me over. I’ve never had an entry reach this milestone before. Thank you so much, everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks like Plato's at it censoring myths and songs again, eh?

+1 (and more if I could)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is really constructive advice. Thanks for giving me some specific things I can work on. I've written up a couple of additions to this the last few days, but nothing really clicked. Maybe I'll try out some of your suggestions after I give it some thought!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The format screw dropped my jaw. I think this is one of those creative connections where the root idea of it alone is enough to warrant an upvote, the execution being supplemental.

That’s also my only criticism though, which is not really a criticism at all: it relies too heavily on the brilliance of the root analogy. I feel that a bit more development — for example, the extension of the classically florid language into the actual documentation side of things, or the substitution of the redaction of SCP-4000-1 with a description (…unless I’m missing something that inherently necessitates the censoring of SCP-4000-1…), or some element of the underworld that gives a spice of some horror — would have made this even better imo.

In any case, this SCP is sure to become…a classic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After the death of Scholar Megara, Scholar Herakles has been assigned to the duty of Gold level beings as punishment for his recklessness until further notice.

+1 for Proto-lolFoundation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
- idk what to put here

Lead = Safe
Silver = Euclid
Gold = Keter
The other one = Thaumiel

Example 1 is Pandora's box

Example 2 is Pithagoras discovering math stuff

Example 3 is what I believe is Hydra (anyone feel free to correct me if I'm wrong)

Example 4… I have no idea. Maybe it is an elusion to the nectar of the gods (thus why it is the Greeks' version of Thaumiel)

Example 5 explains that Hercules accidentally let Megara die (most likely a containment breach that could have been easily avoidable) and how he has been reprimanded by handling keter-like entities, so probably stuff like the Minotaur or Medusa and all that jazz.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am such a sucker for Greek mythology, and equally sucker for a good high concept memetic scip. This was so inspired, my god. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is great. I was actually making a draft for the 4000 contest that would also have that connection between the Foundation and Titanomachy, but after reading this I think I'd rather just keep it as a draft until after this contest is over because frankly this one deserves to win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can always count on some neat format screws arriving during the X000 Contests, and here we have a great one for 4000. You really make a good use of the Socratic dialogue as a means of conveying the information, and the implications of Greek Philosophers serving as SCP precursors is quite creative.

+1. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had one of the biggest grins on my face the whole time I was reading this. Concept stuff and the idea that the Olympians ran a Foundation equivalent is too good of an idea for me not to like.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Reminds me of reading Plato in college. LOVE the heavy use of Platonic Concepts. Wouldn't mind seeing a bit more "exploration" of how exploring them works.
